question: What are the main differences between JUnit 4 and JUnit 5?
interviewerIntent: Check if the candidate has hands-on JUnit 5 experience or only knows JUnit 4. JUnit 5 is the standard in modern Spring Boot projects.
answer: JUnit 5 is a complete rewrite composed of three modules: JUnit Platform (test runner foundation), JUnit Jupiter (new API), and JUnit Vintage (backward compatibility). Key differences: annotations changed (@Before to @BeforeEach, @After to @AfterEach, @BeforeClass to @BeforeAll), @Test moved to org.junit.jupiter.api, support for nested tests via @Nested, parameterized tests improved with @ParameterizedTest and @ValueSource, @ExtendWith replaces @RunWith, and assertions moved to org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions. JUnit 5 also supports dynamic tests via @TestFactory and has better display names with @DisplayName.
shortAnswer: JUnit 5 is three modules (Platform, Jupiter, Vintage). Main changes: @BeforeEach/@AfterEach, @Nested for grouping, better @ParameterizedTest, @ExtendWith instead of @RunWith.
keyPoints: JUnit 5 = Platform + Jupiter + Vintage
@BeforeEach/@AfterEach replace @Before/@After
@Nested allows test grouping by method or scenario
@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class) replaces @RunWith
@DisplayName improves test reports readability

followUps.question: How do you write parameterized tests in JUnit 5?
followUps.answerHint: @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource, @CsvSource, or @MethodSource to supply arguments.

followUps.question: What is @TestFactory used for?
followUps.answerHint: Generating dynamic tests at runtime — useful when test cases are data-driven and not known at compile time.

mistakesToAvoid: Confusing JUnit 4 and JUnit 5 annotation packages
Forgetting that @BeforeAll/@AfterAll methods must be static unless @TestInstance(PER_CLASS) is used
Using @RunWith instead of @ExtendWith in JUnit 5

question: How do you structure tests for a complex service that has multiple scenarios per method?
interviewerIntent: Assess test organization skills. Good candidates use @Nested classes to group related scenarios, making reports readable.
answer: I use @Nested classes to group test scenarios by method under test. Each public method gets a nested class annotated with @DisplayName describing the method. Inside, individual @Test methods cover happy path, edge cases, and error scenarios. This creates a clear hierarchy in test reports: ServiceName > methodName > should do X when Y. Combined with descriptive @DisplayName on each test, the report reads like a specification. I also use @BeforeEach in the nested class for setup specific to that method's scenarios, avoiding polluting the outer class setup.
shortAnswer: Use @Nested classes per method, @DisplayName for readable reports, @BeforeEach per nested class for focused setup. Creates a spec-like test report.
keyPoints: @Nested class per method under test
@DisplayName at class and method level for BDD-style reports
Inner @BeforeEach only affects that nested group
One assertion per test (or one observable behavior)
Name pattern: should_DoX_when_Y or given_context_when_action_then_result

followUps.question: How deep should you nest test classes?
followUps.answerHint: Two levels maximum: outer = service class, first level = method, second level = scenario group. Deeper nesting hurts readability.

followUps.question: What naming convention do you prefer for test methods?
followUps.answerHint: should_BehaviorUnderTest_WhenStateUnderTest or deveX_quandoY. Consistency within a project matters more than the specific format.

mistakesToAvoid: Flat list of 50+ test methods without grouping
Generic names like testMethod1, testSuccess, testFailure
One giant @BeforeEach that sets up state for unrelated tests

question: What is the difference between @Mock, @Spy, and @InjectMocks in Mockito?
interviewerIntent: Core Mockito knowledge. Candidates who confuse @Mock and @Spy or don't know @InjectMocks have limited practical testing experience.
answer: @Mock creates a full mock — all methods return default values (null, 0, false, empty list) unless stubbed. @Spy creates a partial mock wrapping a real object — methods execute real code unless stubbed. @InjectMocks creates an instance of the class under test and injects @Mock/@Spy fields into it via constructor, setter, or field injection. In practice: use @Mock for dependencies you want full control over, @Spy when you need to verify calls on a real implementation while stubbing only specific methods, and avoid @Spy as a default because it couples tests to implementation details.
shortAnswer: @Mock = full mock (all stubbed). @Spy = real object with selective stubbing. @InjectMocks = the class under test with mocks injected.
keyPoints: @Mock returns safe defaults unless stubbed with when()
@Spy calls real methods unless explicitly stubbed with doReturn()
@InjectMocks requires @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)
Prefer @Mock over @Spy — @Spy couples tests to implementation
Use doReturn() not when() with @Spy to avoid calling real method during stubbing

followUps.question: When would you legitimately use @Spy?
followUps.answerHint: When testing a class with some methods that call other methods of the same class, and you want to stub only the internal calls. Rare — often signals a design issue.

followUps.question: What happens if @InjectMocks cannot inject a dependency?
followUps.answerHint: Mockito silently skips it (no error). The field remains null. This can cause NullPointerExceptions in tests that are hard to trace.

mistakesToAvoid: Using @Spy as default instead of @Mock
Forgetting @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class) and wondering why mocks are null
Using when() to stub a @Spy method — it calls the real method first

question: Walk me through the TDD cycle. How do you apply it in practice on a real task?
interviewerIntent: Distinguish candidates who understand TDD as a design tool from those who think it just means writing tests before code.
answer: TDD follows Red-Green-Refactor: write a failing test that defines the desired behavior (Red), write the minimum code to make it pass (Green), then improve the code without changing behavior (Refactor). In practice on a real task: I start with the simplest case — for a price calculator, the first test might be 'calculateTotal with one item returns item price'. I write the test, watch it fail (confirming the test runs), write just enough code to pass it (even hardcoding the return value initially), then add the next test case that forces real implementation. Each cycle takes 2-5 minutes. The benefit is not just tests — TDD forces you to design the interface before the implementation, which leads to simpler APIs.
shortAnswer: Red (failing test) → Green (minimum code to pass) → Refactor (clean up). Each cycle 2-5 min. TDD is a design tool, not just a testing technique.
keyPoints: Red-Green-Refactor is the cycle, not Red-Red-Red
Start with the simplest case, not edge cases
The failing test must fail for the right reason
Green means minimum code — even hardcoding is valid temporarily
Refactor only when all tests are green

followUps.question: What do you do when TDD slows you down significantly?
followUps.answerHint: For pure algorithmic logic, TDD is fast. For UI or integration-heavy code, I write tests after — TDD has a cost/benefit tradeoff per context.

followUps.question: How does TDD change how you design APIs?
followUps.answerHint: Writing the test first means you consume the API before creating it. This naturally leads to simpler, more focused interfaces because you feel the pain of awkward APIs immediately.

mistakesToAvoid: Writing all tests first, then all implementation — that's not TDD
Writing tests that are too complex before any implementation exists
Skipping the refactor step — this is where design improvement happens
Testing implementation details instead of observable behavior

question: What is the difference between unit tests and integration tests? When do you use each?
interviewerIntent: Check if candidate understands test boundaries and can make pragmatic decisions about test type rather than applying one approach everywhere.
answer: Unit tests test a single class/function in isolation with all dependencies mocked — fast (milliseconds), deterministic, run on every commit. Integration tests test how components interact together — with real database, real HTTP calls, or real message brokers — slower (seconds to minutes), can have external dependencies. I use unit tests for business logic, validation rules, and transformations where I control all inputs. I use integration tests for repositories (to verify JPQL/SQL against a real database), REST controllers (to verify serialization and HTTP semantics), and message consumers (to verify the full message-to-action flow). The key is not quantity but coverage of the right things — unit tests for logic, integration tests for contracts between components.
shortAnswer: Unit = isolated, mocked, milliseconds. Integration = real dependencies, seconds. Unit for logic, integration for component contracts (DB, HTTP, messaging).
keyPoints: Unit tests are fast because nothing external runs
Integration tests verify contracts, not logic
Repository tests need a real database (H2 or Testcontainers)
@SpringBootTest is heavy — prefer @WebMvcTest or @DataJpaTest slices
Test pyramid: many unit, some integration, few E2E

followUps.question: How do you handle database state in integration tests?
followUps.answerHint: @Transactional with rollback, or @Sql scripts, or Testcontainers with a clean database per test class.

followUps.question: What is Testcontainers and when would you use it?
followUps.answerHint: Library that spins up real Docker containers (PostgreSQL, Kafka, Redis) for tests. Use when H2 compatibility issues arise or when the real database behavior is critical to test.

mistakesToAvoid: Using @SpringBootTest for everything — it loads the full context and is slow
Mocking the database in integration tests — defeats the purpose
Ignoring test isolation — tests that depend on order are fragile

question: How do you test a Spring Boot REST controller without starting the full application context?
interviewerIntent: Checks knowledge of Spring Boot test slices — a sign of experience with large test suites that need to stay fast.
answer: @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer — controllers, filters, and MockMvc — without the service or repository layers. You mock the service with @MockBean. This is much faster than @SpringBootTest. Example: @WebMvcTest(OrderController.class) with @MockBean OrderService. Then use MockMvc to perform requests and assert HTTP status, response body, and headers. For JSON assertions, use jsonPath(). This approach tests serialization, validation, HTTP status codes, and controller logic without touching the database.
shortAnswer: @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer. Mock services with @MockBean. Use MockMvc to test HTTP contract without starting full context.
keyPoints: @WebMvcTest is a test slice — much faster than @SpringBootTest
Use @MockBean for services and repositories
MockMvc.perform() tests HTTP request/response cycle
Test HTTP status, response body, headers, and validation errors
jsonPath() for asserting JSON structure

followUps.question: What is @DataJpaTest?
followUps.answerHint: Another test slice that loads only JPA components (repositories, entities). Uses in-memory H2 by default. For testing queries without the full context.

followUps.question: How do you test file upload endpoints?
followUps.answerHint: MockMvc with MockMultipartFile and .multipart() request builder.

mistakesToAvoid: Using @SpringBootTest when @WebMvcTest is sufficient
Forgetting @MockBean for services — controller will fail to instantiate
Not testing 4xx responses — validation and error handling are important

question: What is the test pyramid and how does it guide your test strategy?
interviewerIntent: Assess strategic thinking about testing. Candidates who write mostly E2E tests or skip unit tests have skewed pyramids that cause slow, fragile test suites.
answer: The test pyramid (Mike Cohn) has three layers: unit tests at the base (many, fast, cheap), integration tests in the middle (fewer, slower, more expensive), and E2E tests at the top (fewest, slowest, most expensive). The shape guides investment: most tests should be unit tests because they're fast and give immediate feedback. Integration tests cover component boundaries. E2E tests cover critical user journeys only. An inverted pyramid (mostly E2E) means slow feedback loops and fragile tests that break for unrelated reasons. I aim for roughly 70% unit, 20% integration, 10% E2E in a typical backend service.
shortAnswer: Many unit (fast, cheap) → fewer integration (component boundaries) → few E2E (critical paths only). 70/20/10 ratio. Inverted pyramid = slow, fragile suite.
keyPoints: Unit tests catch logic bugs in milliseconds
Integration tests catch contract bugs between components
E2E tests catch workflow bugs but are slow and fragile
Inverted pyramid is an anti-pattern (ice cream cone)
Each layer tests what the layer below cannot

followUps.question: What is the testing trophy (Kent C. Dodds)?
followUps.answerHint: A variation that emphasizes integration tests over unit tests for frontend — because unit tests of components with mocked dependencies give false confidence.

followUps.question: How do you handle flaky tests?
followUps.answerHint: Quarantine flaky tests immediately, investigate root cause (timing, shared state, external dependency), fix or delete. Flaky tests erode trust in the entire suite.

mistakesToAvoid: Writing only E2E tests — they're the most expensive to maintain
Thinking more tests always means better quality
Ignoring test execution time — a suite that takes 30 minutes is not used

question: How do you use ArgumentCaptor and when is it appropriate?
interviewerIntent: ArgumentCaptor is a key tool for verifying what arguments a method was called with. Tests that don't use it often use any() and miss important assertions.
answer: ArgumentCaptor captures the argument passed to a mock method so you can assert on it. Use it when the SUT creates an object internally and passes it to a dependency — you can't access that object directly, but you can capture it. Example: service creates an EmailDTO and calls emailSender.send(dto). With a captor, I capture dto and assert its fields. Declare with @Captor EmailCaptor captor or ArgumentCaptor.forClass(Email.class), then verify(emailSender).send(captor.capture()), then assertEquals(expected, captor.getValue()). Use it when the content of the argument IS the rule being tested. Avoid it when any() is sufficient and the argument's content is not the point.
shortAnswer: ArgumentCaptor captures arguments passed to mocks for assertion. Use when the argument's content is the rule under test — not as a default replacement for any().
keyPoints: Declare with @Captor or ArgumentCaptor.forClass()
captor.capture() goes inside verify()
captor.getValue() returns the captured argument
captor.getAllValues() for multiple calls
Only use when the argument content is what you're testing

followUps.question: What's wrong with using ArgumentCaptor for every verify()?
followUps.answerHint: It makes tests verbose and couples them to implementation details. When the argument content doesn't matter, any() is cleaner and more resilient to refactoring.

followUps.question: How do you capture arguments in a method called multiple times?
followUps.answerHint: captor.getAllValues() returns a List of all captured arguments in call order.

mistakesToAvoid: Calling captor.getValue() before verify() — it throws
Using captors when any() would suffice — over-specification
Forgetting that captured object may be mutated after capture if mutable

question: How do you apply TDD to code that has external dependencies like a database or HTTP API?
interviewerIntent: TDD with real dependencies is a common challenge. Experienced candidates know how to use ports-and-adapters or test doubles to keep cycles fast.
answer: I use the ports-and-adapters pattern: the core domain logic depends on interfaces (ports), not concrete implementations. TDD the domain logic with fast in-memory fakes or mocks. The adapters (real DB, real HTTP) get separate integration tests. For example, an OrderService depends on OrderRepository (interface). In TDD, I mock the repository to test service logic at millisecond speed. The actual JPA implementation of OrderRepository gets tested separately with @DataJpaTest against a real database. This way TDD cycles stay fast (no database startup), and the real behavior is verified at the integration level. The key insight: mock at the port boundary, not inside the implementation.
shortAnswer: Ports-and-adapters: TDD the domain against interface mocks (fast). Test adapters (DB, HTTP) separately with integration tests. Keeps TDD cycles milliseconds fast.
keyPoints: Define interfaces (ports) for external dependencies
TDD domain logic with mocks/fakes — no database needed
Integration tests verify the real adapter separately
Fakes (in-memory implementations) are better than mocks for complex scenarios
Never mock what you don't own (mock your interface, not the JPA API)

followUps.question: What is the difference between a mock and a fake?
followUps.answerHint: Mock is a test double that records calls and can verify interactions. Fake is a working simplified implementation (e.g., in-memory repository). Fakes are more realistic, mocks are more verifiable.

followUps.question: When would you use Testcontainers instead of mocks for TDD?
followUps.answerHint: When the database behavior is the thing being designed — schema queries, constraints, transactions. But only after the core logic is TDD'd with mocks.

mistakesToAvoid: Starting TDD with a running database — defeats the speed benefit
Mocking concrete classes instead of interfaces
Having 100% mocked tests with no integration tests — gives false confidence

question: How do you decide what NOT to test?
interviewerIntent: A key sign of testing maturity. Over-testing is as harmful as under-testing. Experienced developers know when tests add no value.
answer: I don't test: simple getters/setters with no logic, framework behavior (Spring's dependency injection itself), configuration classes with no branching logic, trivial delegation (a service that just calls a repository with no transformation), and generated code. I also don't test implementation details — HOW code works internally — only observable behavior. The rule is: would this test break if I refactored the implementation without changing behavior? If yes, it's testing implementation, not behavior. I focus on tests where failure would be a meaningful signal. A test that always passes or fails only due to refactoring adds noise, not signal.
shortAnswer: Don't test: getters/setters, framework behavior, trivial delegation, implementation details. Test observable behavior. If refactoring breaks the test without changing behavior, it's a bad test.
keyPoints: Trivial getters/setters are not worth testing
Framework behavior (Spring DI, JPA) is already tested by the framework
Test behavior, not implementation
If test breaks on refactor with no behavior change — delete it
100% coverage is not a goal — meaningful coverage is

followUps.question: How do you handle pressure to achieve 100% code coverage?
followUps.answerHint: Coverage is a floor, not a ceiling. 100% coverage with meaningless assertions gives false confidence. I push back and explain that coverage without quality is noise.

followUps.question: Should you test private methods?
followUps.answerHint: No — test them through the public API. If a private method needs direct testing, it's a signal it should be extracted to its own class.

mistakesToAvoid: Chasing 100% line coverage with trivial tests
Testing private methods directly via reflection
Writing tests that just re-implement the production code logic

question: How do you write and organize parameterized tests in JUnit 5?
interviewerIntent: Parameterized tests are essential for testing multiple input scenarios without code duplication. Candidates who don't know them write many near-identical test methods.
answer: @ParameterizedTest replaces @Test for data-driven tests. Sources: @ValueSource for simple arrays of literals, @CsvSource for multiple parameters inline, @MethodSource for complex objects (references a static method returning Stream<Arguments>), @EnumSource for enum values. Each combination runs as a separate test with its own entry in the report. Use @DisplayName with {0}, {1} placeholders to get readable names per combination. Example: testing a price validator with multiple valid and invalid prices — one @ParameterizedTest with @CsvSource('10.0, true', '-1.0, false', '0.0, false') covers all cases cleanly.
shortAnswer: @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource, @CsvSource, or @MethodSource. Each combination is a separate test run. Use @DisplayName with {0} for readable reports.
keyPoints: @ValueSource for single primitive/string arrays
@CsvSource for multiple parameters per row
@MethodSource for complex object arguments
@NullSource and @NullAndEmptySource for null/empty edge cases
Combine with @DisplayName('Input {0} should return {1}') for readable reports

followUps.question: How do you provide complex objects as test parameters?
followUps.answerHint: @MethodSource referencing a static method that returns Stream<Arguments>. Arguments.of() wraps each set of parameters.

followUps.question: What is @EnumSource useful for?
followUps.answerHint: Running the same test for all or selected enum values. Can include/exclude specific values with names attribute.

mistakesToAvoid: Writing 10 identical test methods for 10 input variations
Using @MethodSource with non-static methods (requires @TestInstance(PER_CLASS))
Complex logic inside @CsvSource strings — extract to @MethodSource

question: How do you test code that throws exceptions? How do you verify both the exception and its message?
interviewerIntent: Exception testing is frequently done wrong — either testing the wrong exception type or not verifying the message/cause.
answer: JUnit 5's assertThrows() is the standard approach: Throwable ex = assertThrows(OrderNotFoundException.class, () -> service.findOrder(-1L)). It returns the exception, so you can then assert on its message, cause, or custom fields: assertEquals('Order -1 not found', ex.getMessage()). For Mockito, when the mock should throw: when(repo.findById(any())).thenThrow(new RuntimeException('DB error')). For void methods: doThrow(new Exception()).when(repo).delete(any()). Avoid the older @Test(expected=X) JUnit 4 approach — it doesn't let you assert on the exception's message or cause.
shortAnswer: assertThrows() returns the exception for further assertion. Verify both type AND message. For mocks throwing: when().thenThrow() or doThrow().when() for void methods.
keyPoints: assertThrows() returns the exception — always capture it
Assert both exception type AND message when message is part of the contract
thenThrow() for non-void mocked methods
doThrow().when() for void mocked methods
assertThrowsExactly() when subclasses should not match

followUps.question: How do you test that no exception is thrown?
followUps.answerHint: assertDoesNotThrow(() -> service.validOperation()). Or simply let the test pass — if no exception occurs, the test passes naturally.

followUps.question: What is the difference between assertThrows and assertThrowsExactly?
followUps.answerHint: assertThrows matches subclasses; assertThrowsExactly requires exact type. Use assertThrowsExactly when the exact exception class is part of the contract.

mistakesToAvoid: Using @Test(expected=X) — cannot assert on exception details
Catching the exception in a try/catch inside the test — masks failures
Not asserting the message when it's part of the API contract

question: How do you test a Kafka consumer or JMS listener in a Spring Boot application?
interviewerIntent: Messaging integration tests are common in backend services. Candidates who know Testcontainers and Spring's test support stand out.
answer: For Kafka: use @EmbeddedKafka (Spring Kafka test support) for fast in-memory testing, or Testcontainers with a real Kafka image for full fidelity. Publish a message via KafkaTemplate in the test, then use CountDownLatch or Awaitility to wait for the consumer to process it (async by nature), then assert the side effect (database record, service call). For JMS with ActiveMQ Artemis: use an embedded broker or Testcontainers, send a message via JmsTemplate, wait with Awaitility, assert the effect. Key pattern: test the observable side effect, not the internal consumer method.
shortAnswer: @EmbeddedKafka or Testcontainers for Kafka. Send message via template, use Awaitility to wait for async processing, assert side effect. Test the effect, not the listener method.
keyPoints: @EmbeddedKafka for fast Kafka tests without Docker
Testcontainers when real broker behavior matters
Awaitility for polling async conditions without Thread.sleep()
Test the side effect (DB record, downstream call), not the listener method directly
Use @DirtiesContext if the broker state needs reset between tests

followUps.question: What is Awaitility and why use it over Thread.sleep()?
followUps.answerHint: Awaitility polls a condition until it's true or times out. Thread.sleep() is a fixed delay that's either too short (flaky) or too long (slow). Awaitility is faster and more reliable.

followUps.question: How do you test dead-letter queue behavior?
followUps.answerHint: Send a message that will cause the consumer to throw, configure max retries to a small number (like 1), then assert the message ends up in the DLQ topic/queue.

mistakesToAvoid: Thread.sleep() in async tests — flaky and slow
Testing the @KafkaListener method directly without a real message — misses deserialization and headers
Not resetting consumer offsets between tests — causes message replay

question: How do you introduce tests into a legacy codebase with no existing test coverage?
interviewerIntent: A practical challenge in every enterprise team. Candidates who know characterization tests and the strangler fig pattern show real-world experience.
answer: I follow a safe approach: first, identify the riskiest parts — code that changes frequently and has no tests. Second, write characterization tests (also called approval tests) — tests that document current behavior without judgment. These fail if behavior changes, giving a safety net. Third, when modifying code, write a test for the specific behavior I'm changing BEFORE changing it — so I know the test was green, then red after my change, then green again. Fourth, extract dependencies to interfaces to enable mocking. I avoid trying to get 100% coverage of legacy code immediately — instead, test grows as code is touched. The Strangler Fig pattern: add tests at the seams (public API entry points) first.
shortAnswer: Characterization tests first (document current behavior). Add tests for what you're about to change. Extract interfaces to enable mocking. Grow coverage as code is touched, not all at once.
keyPoints: Characterization tests capture current behavior, not desired behavior
Write test BEFORE touching legacy code, not after
Seam points: public methods, interface boundaries
Avoid @Spy on legacy classes — extract interfaces instead
Don't aim for 100% legacy coverage — focus on high-risk, high-change areas

followUps.question: What is Michael Feathers' definition of a 'seam'?
followUps.answerHint: A place in code where behavior can be changed without modifying the code in that place — typically an interface, constructor parameter, or method override point.

followUps.question: How do you handle static method calls in legacy code that need to be tested?
followUps.answerHint: Wrap the static call in an instance method on a dependency that can be mocked. Or use PowerMock (last resort). Ideally, remove the static coupling.

mistakesToAvoid: Refactoring legacy code before writing tests — you have no safety net
Writing tests that pass by checking what the broken code does
Trying to test everything at once — causes analysis paralysis

question: What is the AAA pattern in unit testing and why does it matter?
interviewerIntent: Basic test structure knowledge. Tests without clear structure are hard to read and maintain. AAA is the industry standard.
answer: AAA stands for Arrange-Act-Assert. Arrange: set up the preconditions and inputs (create objects, stub mocks, define expected values). Act: invoke the method under test — ideally one line. Assert: verify the outcome (return value, exception thrown, or side effect via verify()). The pattern matters because it makes tests readable as mini-specifications: here's the context, here's the action, here's what should happen. Tests that mix arrangement and assertion throughout are confusing. I enforce AAA with blank lines or comments between sections. The Act section should be exactly one call — if it's multiple calls, you're testing a workflow, not a unit.
shortAnswer: Arrange (setup) → Act (one call) → Assert (verify outcome). Makes tests readable as specs. Act = one line. Mix of all three = hard to read.
keyPoints: Arrange: all setup before the action
Act: single method invocation — one line
Assert: verify outcome, not implementation
Blank lines between sections improve readability
Given-When-Then is equivalent BDD terminology

followUps.question: What do you do when the Arrange section becomes very large?
followUps.answerHint: Extract to a helper/builder method or @BeforeEach. If Arrange is always large, consider an Object Mother or Builder pattern for test data.

followUps.question: Is it acceptable to have multiple Assert lines?
followUps.answerHint: Yes, when they all verify the same observable outcome. One Assert PER BEHAVIOR, not one Assert per method call.

mistakesToAvoid: Multiple Act invocations in one test — tests multiple things
Assertions mixed into Arrange section
Using verify() when the return value is the real assertion

question: What should not be mocked in an integration test?
answer:
I avoid mocking components whose real integration behavior is what the test must verify.

For a repository test, I prefer a real database environment, often using Testcontainers. For an HTTP integration test, I exercise serialization, validation, security and persistence together.

I mock only external boundaries that are outside the scope of the test.

question: What is the difference between a unit test and a component test?
answer:
A unit test isolates a small unit of behavior and normally runs without infrastructure.

A component test exercises a deployable service or meaningful application slice through its public interface while replacing external systems.

Component tests give more confidence than heavily mocked unit tests while remaining faster and more stable than full end-to-end tests.

question: How do you test Kafka consumers?
answer:
I test the business handler independently first.

Then I add an integration test with a real Kafka-compatible environment to verify serialization, topic configuration, consumer behavior and offset handling.

I also test duplicate delivery, retry behavior, malformed messages and dead-letter routing.

question: What makes a test flaky?
answer:
A flaky test depends on uncontrolled conditions such as timing, execution order, shared state, real clocks, random data, external networks or asynchronous operations without deterministic synchronization.

The solution is not usually more retries. The solution is controlling those dependencies.
